description: Colin Kaepernick's kneeling during the national anthem (2016) sparked a polarizing debate. Support for protests was initially ~35%, peaked ~45% after Trump's 'SOB' comments in September 2017 triggered solidarity kneeling, then settled ~40%. Tests elite provocation and counter-mobilization dynamics.

key_events.description: Kaepernick begins kneeling during national anthem (August 2016). Initial reaction heavily negative, especially among older white demographics.
Other NFL players join protests. Eric Reid, Brandon Marshall kneel. Movement grows but Kaepernick goes unsigned by any NFL team.
Trump calls kneeling players 'sons of bitches' at Alabama rally (Sept 22, 2017). Massive solidarity response: 200+ players kneel the following Sunday.
Peak solidarity: entire teams kneel or link arms. NFL owners join protests. Support briefly reaches ~45%. Framing shifts from flag to free speech.
NFL implements anthem policy requiring standing. Protests fade from visibility. Support settles at ~40%, a net gain of ~12pts from start.

calibration_notes: Tests elite provocation and counter-mobilization. Key patterns: initial minority position (~28% support), slow organic growth, Trump's attack backfired by generating solidarity and media attention (net +9pts in one week), but effect partially reversed as partisan sorting resumed, racial opinion gap enormous (Black Americans ~80% support vs White ~30%), sport/patriotism framing vs racial justice framing determined position. Reuters/Ipsos, Quinnipiac, Seton Hall poll data used.
